evidence: High-import hub modules bind governance policy at import time: `runtimeErrorAliasPolicy = getDeprecationPolicy('runtime_error_alias')` in `src/shared/lib/errorHandling/handleError.ts` and `legacySupabaseFacadePolicy = getDeprecationPolicy('legacy_supabase_facade')` in `src/integrations/supabase/legacy/legacySupabaseFacade.ts`.

Deprecated compatibility surfaces are still colocated with active runtime entry points (`reportRuntimeError` in `handleError.ts`, `getLegacySupabaseClient` in `legacySupabaseFacade.ts`) with explicit removal target `2026-06-30`, indicating ongoing dual-path boundary blur.

`src/shared/contexts/ProjectContext.tsx` writes runtime state to `window.__PROJECT_CONTEXT__`, while gallery/lightbox state is coordinated across multiple hooks/components (`useMediaGalleryItemState`, `InfoTooltip`, `ShotActions`, `useMobileInteractions`) through prop/ref choreography rather than a single subsystem boundary.

Outcome contracts are fragmented across the same batch: `supabase/functions/_shared/edgeOperation.ts` uses `{ ok, value|errorCode }`, `src/tools/travel-between-images/components/ShotEditor/services/generateVideoService.ts` uses `{ success, error? }`, and `src/shared/lib/media/safePlay.ts` collapses failures to `boolean`.

`src/shared/lib/toolSettingsService.ts` mixes throw-based failure (`ToolSettingsError`) with silent success fallback to defaults for some auth/network-adjacent states, so callers cannot rely on one error channel.
`supabase/functions/_shared/edgeOperation.ts` exports parallel alias families (`Edge*` and `Operation*`) for the same result types, creating multiple canonical names for one API surface.

findings.summary: Governance policy lookup runs at import time in high-fan-in core modules.
evidence: `handleError.ts` computes `runtimeErrorAliasPolicy` at module load via `getDeprecationPolicy(...)`.
`legacySupabaseFacade.ts` computes `legacySupabaseFacadePolicy` at module load via `getDeprecationPolicy(...)`.
Both files are listed as heavily imported hubs in the packet context, so import-time coupling amplifies startup and boundary fragility.
findings.suggestion: Move governance resolution behind call-time boundaries (inside deprecated functions) or inject policy from a dedicated governance adapter. Keep hot-path runtime helpers free of governance imports to avoid global initialization coupling.

findings.summary: Legacy compatibility APIs remain on primary module entrypoints instead of isolated legacy boundaries.
evidence: `handleError.ts` exports both canonical APIs and deprecated alias `reportRuntimeError` in the same module.
`legacySupabaseFacade.ts` is explicitly transitional and deprecated, but still serves as an active compatibility accessor.
Both include future removal target `2026-06-30`, showing migration is unresolved while these symbols stay in high-traffic import surfaces.
findings.suggestion: Split deprecated exports into dedicated `legacy/` entrypoints and keep canonical modules strict. Add boundary guardrails (lint/import restriction) to block new imports from legacy paths and force migration through explicit adapter modules.

findings.summary: Media gallery interaction state is scattered across hooks/components with cross-module mutable choreography.
evidence: `useMediaGalleryItemState.ts` exposes many mutable setters (`setIsInfoOpen`, `setIsShotSelectorOpen`, `setIsCreateShotModalOpen`, etc.) for other modules to coordinate.
`InfoTooltip.tsx` and `ShotActions.tsx` each depend on externally managed open/selection state callbacks, creating distributed UI-state ownership.
`useMobileInteractions.ts` introduces additional mutable refs/timeouts and global event listeners for related interaction behavior.
`ProjectContext.tsx` also uses a global window bridge (`window.__PROJECT_CONTEXT__`) for fallback coordination, reinforcing subsystem-level shared mutable state patterns.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single gallery interaction controller (reducer/state machine) that owns tooltip/selector/popover/lightbox transitions and exposes declarative events. Remove ad-hoc setter/ref passing and eliminate global fallback channels for interaction/state handoff.

findings.summary: Service APIs expose incompatible success/failure shapes, forcing per-call adapters.
evidence: `edgeOperation` defines a discriminated union with `ok: true/false`, `errorCode`, and `recoverable`.
`generateVideoService` returns `{ success: boolean, error?: string }` and drops structured error metadata in `failureResult`.
`safePlay` returns only `Promise<boolean>`, so callers cannot inspect reason/category for playback failures.
`useTaskStatusCounts` encodes partial failure as domain fields (`degraded`, `failedQueries`, `errorCode`) instead of a shared result envelope.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one discriminated result contract (for example `{ ok, value } | { ok, errorCode, message, recoverable, cause }`) for service-layer boundaries and add small adapter shims at UI edges only.

findings.summary: Tool settings fetch mixes exceptions with silent fallback success, making failure semantics unpredictable.
evidence: `fetchToolSettingsSupabase` throws `ToolSettingsError` for many failures (`auth_required`, `network`, `scope_fetch_failed`) but returns successful default settings for some auth-timeout string paths.
In contrast, `checkRateLimit` consistently returns `OperationResult` and marks fail-open behavior inside the returned payload (`degraded.reason`) without switching channels.
findings.suggestion: Use one error channel for tool settings: either always return a typed `OperationResult` (including a degraded/defaults mode with reason codes) or always throw typed errors, but avoid mixing both in one API.

findings.summary: Edge operation result types expose duplicate naming families for identical contracts.
evidence: `edgeOperation.ts` exports both `EdgeOperationResult` and `OperationResult` aliases (and matching success/failure aliases) for the same shape.
Downstream files import the `Operation*` names, while the same module also advertises `Edge*` names, leaving two valid public vocabularies for one API.
findings.suggestion: Choose a single canonical naming family (`Operation*` or `Edge*`), deprecate the other with a removal window, and update imports to one consistent surface.

findings.summary: Deprecated compatibility APIs remain first-class module boundaries, so contract enforcement is mostly advisory.
evidence: README.md describes governance gates and removal targets, but runtime still exports deprecated APIs directly.
legacySupabaseFacade exposes `getLegacySupabaseClient()` and only signals via `signalPastRemovalTargetUsage(...)` before returning the runtime client.
handleError.ts keeps `reportRuntimeError(...)` as a forwarding alias with deprecation metadata, leaving two importable paths for the same boundary behavior.
findings.suggestion: Convert deprecation contracts into enforceable architecture controls: add lint/build-time restricted-import rules for deprecated paths, add a staged runtime hard-fail mode before the removal date, and remove deprecated exports once import budget reaches zero so only canonical entry points remain.

findings.summary: Single canonical type barrel mixes generation and shot domains, creating a broad cross-module coupling hub.
evidence: README.md declares `@/types/generationAndShots` as canonical after compatibility-shim removal.

src/types/generationAndShots.ts combines re-exports from separate domains (`generation*` and `shot`) behind one import surface, forcing shared dependency direction through a single high-fan-in module.
findings.suggestion: Split the canonical surface into domain barrels (for example `@/types/generation` and `@/types/shots`) and keep a temporary compatibility index with explicit deprecation metadata and import-budget checks, so modules depend only on their domain boundary.
